content.text: The Role of Human Evaluation

Human evaluation is the ground truth layer of every agent evaluation system. LLM judges are fast and cheap, but they inherit the model's blind spots and biases. A human annotator can recognise when an agent's response is technically correct but unhelpful in context, catches cultural inappropriateness that a judge model trained on a different corpus would miss, and can flag novel failure modes that no automated metric was designed to detect. Use human eval sparingly and purposefully — it is expensive — but treat it as the source of truth that calibrates every other signal.

attrs.alt: Human evaluation workflow: sample cases, assign to annotators, collect ratings or comparisons, adjudicate disagreements, aggregate and feed back to CI

content.text: When to Use Human Evaluation
Calibrating LLM judges: run human eval on a sample of the same cases your judge model scores; measure correlation to verify the judge is trustworthy before scaling it
Safety audits: automated safety classifiers have known gaps; human annotators trained on policy catch edge cases the classifier misses
New capability launches: before releasing a new tool or agent mode, human eval on a targeted sample provides confidence that the classifier score means what you think it means
Incident investigation: when a production failure is reported, human review of the specific trajectory is often the fastest way to diagnose the root cause
Preference data collection: training reward models or running DPO requires human preference labels — you cannot generate these fully automatically
Annotation Formats
Absolute Rating

Each annotator rates the agent response on a rubric independently. Ratings are on a small integer scale (0–2 or 0–3) per criterion, then aggregated. Absolute rating produces per-criterion quality scores and is best for understanding depth of quality.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from statistics import mean

@dataclass
class RubricCriterion:
    name: str
    description: str
    min_score: int = 0
    max_score: int = 3

@dataclass
class AbsoluteAnnotation:
    case_id: str
    annotator_id: str
    scores: dict[str, int]    # criterion name → score
    notes: str = ""

SUPPORT_RUBRIC = [
    RubricCriterion("accuracy",     "Is the information factually correct?"),
    RubricCriterion("completeness", "Does the response address all parts of the request?"),
    RubricCriterion("tone",         "Is the tone appropriate for customer support?"),
    RubricCriterion("clarity",      "Is the response easy to understand?"),
]

def normalised_score(annotation: AbsoluteAnnotation, rubric: list[RubricCriterion]) -> float:
    """Return 0.0-1.0 composite from raw criterion scores."""
    by_name = {c.name: c for c in rubric}
    total, max_possible = 0, 0
    for criterion in rubric:
        raw = annotation.scores.get(criterion.name, 0)
        total += raw
        max_possible += criterion.max_score
    return total / max_possible if max_possible else 0.0
Pairwise Comparison

Annotators are shown two responses (A and B) for the same input and choose which is better, or mark them as equal. Pairwise comparison is easier for annotators than absolute rating because relative judgements are more natural than absolute ones. It is the right format for preference data used in RLHF pipelines.
Error Flagging

Annotators mark specific error types present in the response. This format is most efficient for safety audits and error taxonomy development — annotators scan for predefined failure modes rather than assigning a holistic quality score.
Inter-Annotator Agreement

Before trusting human eval scores, measure inter-annotator agreement (IAA). IAA tells you whether different annotators are interpreting the rubric consistently. Low IAA means the rubric is ambiguous, not that the agent quality is variable. Cohen's kappa is the standard measure for categorical and ordinal judgements.
Adjudication

When annotators disagree significantly (e.g., one scores a response 0 and another scores it 3), the disagreement must be resolved before using the label. Adjudication involves a senior annotator reviewing both ratings and the original case and making a final determination. Record adjudication decisions to build a calibration set for future annotators.
Connecting Human Eval to Automated Systems
Human eval is most valuable when its output feeds back into automated systems. Three connections make this loop complete:
Judge calibration: compute Spearman correlation between human scores and LLM judge scores on the same cases; if correlation falls below 0.70, retrain or re-prompt the judge
Regression dataset growth: every human-reviewed failure case becomes a new entry in the offline eval dataset, permanently raising the automated bar
Reward model training: pairwise annotations in the preference format can be used directly to train a reward model or for DPO fine-tuning
Designing Annotation Tasks for Efficiency

The biggest bottleneck in human evaluation is annotator time. A poorly designed annotation task — too many criteria, ambiguous definitions, unclear instructions — wastes that time and produces noisy data. Good annotation task design follows a few proven principles.
Limit to 3-4 criteria per task: annotators lose consistency after the fourth criterion; use separate tasks for quality and safety rather than combining them
Provide concrete examples in the rubric: for each score level (0, 1, 2, 3), show one example response so annotators calibrate to examples rather than descriptions
Pilot with two annotators first: before scaling to a full annotation batch, run 20-30 cases with two experienced annotators and measure kappa; only scale if kappa >= 0.60
Show only what annotators need: hide the system prompt if it would bias the annotator; show it if the task requires understanding the agent's instructions
Time each annotation: if the median annotation takes less than 30 seconds, the task may be too coarse; if it takes more than 3 minutes, split it into subtasks
Sampling Strategy for Human Eval

Random sampling from production logs is the baseline, but targeted sampling produces more useful data per annotation dollar. Prioritise cases that are most likely to reveal quality gaps or calibrate automated systems.

import random
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SamplingConfig:
    random_fraction: float = 0.30    # 30% random baseline
    low_score_fraction: float = 0.40  # 40% cases with low automated scores
    high_score_fraction: float = 0.10 # 10% high-score cases (sanity check)
    new_query_fraction: float = 0.20  # 20% queries not seen in eval set before

def sample_for_human_eval(
    cases: list[dict],
    automated_scores: dict[str, float],
    eval_set_ids: set[str],
    target_n: int,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Sample target_n cases using a stratified strategy.
    Each case dict must have an 'id' key.
    automated_scores: case_id -> float (0.0-1.0)
    eval_set_ids: set of ids already in the eval dataset
    """
    LOW_THRESHOLD  = 0.60
    HIGH_THRESHOLD = 0.90

    low_score   = [c for c in cases if automated_scores.get(c["id"], 1.0) < LOW_THRESHOLD]
    high_score  = [c for c in cases if automated_scores.get(c["id"], 0.0) > HIGH_THRESHOLD]
    new_queries = [c for c in cases if c["id"] not in eval_set_ids]
    remaining   = [c for c in cases if c not in low_score + high_score + new_queries]

    config = SamplingConfig()
    n_low   = int(target_n * config.low_score_fraction)
    n_high  = int(target_n * config.high_score_fraction)
    n_new   = int(target_n * config.new_query_fraction)
    n_rand  = target_n - n_low - n_high - n_new

    sampled = (
        random.sample(low_score,   min(n_low,  len(low_score)))
        + random.sample(high_score,  min(n_high, len(high_score)))
        + random.sample(new_queries, min(n_new,  len(new_queries)))
        + random.sample(remaining,   min(n_rand, len(remaining)))
    )
    return sampled[:target_n]
Building the Annotation Context Packet

Annotators make better judgements when they understand the agent's intended role and constraints. Provide a context packet with each annotation task that gives annotators the minimum information they need — no more, no less.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AnnotationContextPacket:
    case_id: str
    agent_role: str           # e.g. "Customer support agent for an e-commerce platform"
    user_input: str
    agent_response: str
    relevant_policy: str      # excerpt from policy the agent should follow
    rubric_criteria: list[dict]  # [{name, description, levels: [{score, example}]}]
    # Optionally include for trajectory eval
    tool_calls_summary: str = ""

def format_packet_for_annotator(packet: AnnotationContextPacket) -> str:
    """Render the context packet as a readable string for the annotation UI."""
    lines = [
        f"AGENT ROLE: {packet.agent_role}",
        f"",
        f"USER MESSAGE:",
        f"  {packet.user_input}",
        f"",
        f"AGENT RESPONSE:",
        f"  {packet.agent_response}",
        f"",
        f"RELEVANT POLICY:",
        f"  {packet.relevant_policy}",
    ]
    if packet.tool_calls_summary:
        lines += ["", f"TOOL CALLS MADE:", f"  {packet.tool_calls_summary}"]
    return "\n".join(lines)
Summary

Human evaluation provides the ground truth that calibrates every other evaluation method. Use it in three formats: absolute rating for quality depth, pairwise comparison for preference data, and error flagging for safety audits. Measure inter-annotator agreement with Cohen's kappa before trusting scores — target kappa ≥ 0.60. Connect human eval output back to your automated systems by using it to calibrate LLM judges, grow the offline eval dataset, and supply preference pairs for reward model training.
